vocabulary.definition: the region of the body between the chest and the pelvis
腹部的 e.g. abdominal pain; abdominal swelling; abdominal X-ray
very different from usual in a way that seems strange, worrying, wrong, or dangerous
vocabulary.cn: 不正常的﹐反常的﹔异常的

vocabulary.definition: a physical deformity or malformation, a behavioral or mental problem, or a variation from normal in the structure or function of a cell, tissue, or organ in the body
also called a graze, a wound on the skin surface that is caused by scraping or rubbing
the process by which fluids or other substances are taken up by body tissues
adjustment, especially the process by which the eye adjusts itself to focus on near objects
The colourless, pungent, organic acid that gives vinegar its sour taste. In medicine, acetic acid is an ingredient of antiseptic gels that are used for certain vaginal infections.
a type of neurotransmitter(a chemical that transmits messages between nerve cells or between nerve and muscle cells)
the tendon that raises the heel
a combination of mechanisms that ensures that the body's fluids are neither too acid nor too alkaline
a disturbance of the body's acid–base balance in which there is an accumulation of acid or loss of alkali (base)
a chronic skin disorder caused by inflammation of the hair follicles and sebaceous glands in the skin
relating to sound and the way people hear things
a bony prominence at the top of the scapula (shoulderblade)
the common abbreviation for adrenocorticotrophic hormone (also called corticotrophin)
a protein involved in muscle contraction, in which microscopic filaments of actin and another protein, myosin, slide in between each other
vocabulary.cn: 肌动蛋白, 肌纤蛋白

vocabulary.definition: a branch of Chinese medicine in which needles are inserted into a patient's skin as therapy for various disorders or to induce anaesthesia
a term often used to describe a disorder or symptom that develops suddenly. Acute conditions may or may not be severe, and they are usually of short duration.
the abbreviation for attention deficit disorder, more commonly known as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder
dependence on, and craving for, a particular drug, for example alcohol, diazepam (a tranquillizer), or heroin
a mass of glandular tissue at the back of the nasal passage above the tonsils
the joining of normally unconnected body parts by bands of fibrous tissue

A substance that enhances the action of another substance in the body. The term is usually used to describe an ingredient added to a vaccine to increase the production of antibodies by the immune system, thus enhancing the vaccine's effect.
the period between childhood and adulthood, which broadly corresponds to the teenage year
insufficient production of hormones by the adrenal cortex (the outer part of the adrenal glands)
a pair of small, triangular endocrine glands located above the kidneys
(also adrenalin) a hormone, also called epinephrine, released by the adrenal glands in response to signals from the sympathetic autonomic nervous system
requiring oxygen to live, function, and grow
exercises, such as swimming and cycling, that allow muscles to work at a steady rate with a constant, adequate supply of oxygen-carrying blood, and that can therefore be sustained for long periods
a term used to describe a person's mood
vocabulary.cn: （尤指影响行为或行动的）情感, 感情

vocabulary.definition: a term used to describe the attraction between chemicals that causes them to bind together, as, for example, between an antigen and an antibody
any substance or force capable of bringing about a biological, chemical, or physical change
a general term for a wide variety of acts of hostility
having a stimulating effect. An agonist drug, sometimes known as an activator, is one that binds to a sensory nerve cell (receptor) and triggers or increases a particular activity in that cell.

a collective term for the passages through which air enters and leaves the lungs (see respiratory system). The term is also applied to a tube inserted into the mouth of an unconscious person to prevent the tongue from obstructing breathing.
the most abundant protein in the blood plasma. Albumin is made in the liver from amino acids.
feeling like a stranger, even when among familiar people or places, and being unable to identify with a culture, family, or peer group

also known as a base, an alkali is chemically defined as a donor of hydroxyl ions (each of which comprises an atom of hydrogen linked to an atom of oxygen and has an overall negative electrical charge)
a disturbance of the body's acid–base balance in which there is an accumulation of alkali or a loss of acid
various conditions caused by inappropriate or exaggerated reactions of the immune system (known as hypersensitivity reactions) to a variety of substances
a normally harmless substance that causes an allergic reaction
any medical system based on a theory of disease or method of treatment other than the orthodox science of Western medicine
vocabulary.cn: 替代医学；边缘医学；另类疗法

vocabulary.definition: (also mountain sickness) an illness that can affect people who have ascended rapidly to heights above 2,400m–3,000m.
a progressive condition in which nerve cells in the brain degenerate and the brain shrinks. Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia.
vocabulary.cn: 阿尔茨海默病﹐早老性痴呆病

vocabulary.definition: a group of chemical compounds that form the basic structural units of all proteins
loss of ability to memorize information and/or to recall information stored in memory

shrinkage or wasting away of a muscle, leading to weakness. Amyotrophy is usually due to poor nutrition, reduced use of the muscle (as when a limb is immobilized for a long period), or disruption of the blood or nerve supply to the muscle (as can occur in diabetes mellitus or poliomyelitis).
the manufacture of complex molecules, such as fats and proteins, from simpler molecules by metabolic processes in living cells
(anaemia) a condition in which the concentration of the oxygen-carrying pigment haemoglobin in the blood is below normal
capable of living, functioning, and growing without oxygen
(also anaesthesia) Absence of all sensation; insensibility. The term most commonly refers to anaesthesia that is induced artificially for medical purposes.
drugs used to relieve pain
the structure of the body of any living thing, and its scientific study
a deviation from what is accepted as normal, especially a birth defect such as a limb malformation
loss of appetite
Having an opposing effect. For example, antagonist drugs counteract the effects of naturally occurring chemicals in the body.
a group of drugs used to treat infections caused by bacteria and to prevent bacterial infection in cases of immune system impairment
a protein that is made by certain lymphocytes (white blood cells) to neutralize an antigen (foreign protein) in the body
drugs used in the treatment of depression
a substance that neutralizes or counteracts the effects of a poison
a substance that can trigger an immune response, resulting in production of an antibody as part of the body's defense against infection and disease
a type of chemical that neutralizes potentially damaging oxidizing molecules known as free radicals
chemicals applied to the skin in order to destroy bacteria and other microorganisms, thereby preventing infection

a preparation containing antibodies (also known as immunoglobulins) that combine with specific antigens (foreign proteins), usually components of microorganisms, leading to deactivation or destruction of the microorganisms

a complete absence of previously acquired language skills, caused by a brain disorder that affects the ability to speak and write, and/or the ability to comprehend and read. Related disabilities that may occur in aphasia are alexia (word blindness) and agraphia (writing impairment)
a small, narrow tube that projects out of the caecum (the 1st part of the colon) at the lower right-hand side of the abdomen
acute inflammation of the appendix
a form of complementary medicine that uses aromatic oils extracted from plants
a blood vessel that carries blood away from the heart
inflammation of one or more joints, with pain, swelling, and stiffness
a rare developmental disorder that is usually first recognized in childhood because of difficulties with social interactions, stilted speech, and very specialized interests
vocabulary.cn: 阿斯波哥尔综合征〔一种精神疾病﹐表现为社交困难并仅对单一事物有强烈兴趣〕

vocabulary.definition: a nonopioid analgesic drug used to treat disorders such as headache, menstrual pain, and muscle discomfort
analysis or measurement of a substance to determine its presence or effects
a lung disease in which there is intermittent narrowing of the bronchi (airways), causing shortness of breath, wheezing, and cough
a medical term meaning without symptoms. For example, hypertension is often asymptomatic and is usually discovered during a routine blood pressure test.
a common condition in which the skin between the toes becomes itchy and sore, and may crack, peel, or blister
Either of the 2 (right and left) upper chambers of the heart that collect blood from the body and lungs respectively. The atria open directly into the ventricles.
wasting away or shrinkage of a normally developed tissue or organ due to a reduction in the size or number of its cells
vocabulary.cn: （身体组织或器官的）衰退, 萎缩; 退化

vocabulary.definition: a peculiar 'warning' sensation that precedes or marks the onset of a migraine attack or of a seizure in epilepsy
vocabulary.cn: （癫痫或偏头痛发作前的）先兆, 预感

vocabulary.definition: a rare condition in which a child is unable to relate to people and situations

A common name for a mild form of depression that sometimes occurs in women after childbirth. Baby blues almost always disappears without treatment but can occasionally develop into a more serious depressive illness (see postnatal depression).
(pl. bacteria) single-celled microorganisms that are invisible to the naked eye
a strip or tube of fabric used to keep dressings in position, to apply pressure, to control bleeding, or to support a sprain or strain
vocabulary.cn: （用于包扎伤口或保护受伤部位的）绷带

vocabulary.definition: procedures used to detect and follow the progress of some gastrointestinal tract disorders
a cream used to protect the skin against the effects of irritant substances and of excessive exposure to water
a term used to describe a disease that is relatively harmless
the name given to a muscle originating as 2 separate parts, which then fuse
a term that means affecting both sides of the body, or affecting both organs if they are paired (for example, both ears in bilateral deafness)
A greenish-brown alkaline liquid secreted by the liver. Bile carries away waste products formed in the liver and also helps to break down fats in the small intestine for digestion.
the organs and ducts by which bile is formed, concentrated, and carried from the liver to the duodenum (the first part of the small intestine)
the proportion of a drug that reaches the target organs and tissues, usually expressed as a percentage of the dose administered
vocabulary.cn: 生物药效率, 生物利用度

vocabulary.definition: a science that studies the chemistry of living organisms
a discipline that applies engineering methods and principles to the body to explain how it functions and to treat disorders
a diagnostic test in which a small amount of tissue or cells are removed from the body for microscopic examination. It is an accurate method of diagnosing many illnesses, including cancer.
Physiological functions that vary in a rhythmic way. Most biorhythms are based on a daily, or circadian (24-hour), cycle.
the use of living organisms such as bacteria in industry and science
a vitamin of the B complex that is essential for the breakdown of fats
vocabulary.cn: 生物素, 维生素H, 辅酶R

vocabulary.definition: a common term for loss of consciousness
vocabulary.cn: 短暂意识丧失, 短暂失去知觉

vocabulary.definition: the hollow, muscular organ in the lower abdomen that acts as a reservoir for urine
a cell cluster that develops from a fertilized ovum and grows into an embryo
a type of fungal infection that can affect the lungs and other internal organs
loss of blood from the circulatory system caused by damage to the blood vessels or by a bleeding disorder
a collection of fluid beneath the outer layer of the skin that forms a raised area
cells, also called blood corpuscles, present in blood for most or part of their lifespan

the process of blood solidification. Clotting is important in stemming bleeding from damaged blood vessels. However, unwanted blood clotting can occur inside major blood vessels and cause a myocardial infarction (heart attack) or stroke.
a test, also called full blood count, that measures haemoglobin concentration and the numbers of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets in 1 cu. mm of blood
vocabulary.cn: 全血细胞计数；血常规；血液细胞计数

vocabulary.definition: Systems of classifying blood according to the different marker proteins (antigens) on the surface of red blood cells and antibodies in the plasma. These antigens affect the ability of the red blood cells to provoke an immune response. There are 2 main blood grouping systems: the ABO system and the rhesus system.
the infusion of large volumes of blood or blood products directly into the bloodstream to remedy severe blood loss or to correct chronic anaemia
a general term given to arteries, veins, and capillaries (the circulatory system)
indistinct or fuzzy visual images
An indicator of healthy body weight. BMI is calculated by dividing the weight in kilograms by the square of the height in meters. The normal range is 20–25.
the compactness of bone tissue in relation to its volume. A decrease in bone density is a normal part of aging.
the soft fatty tissue that is found in bone cavities; it may be red or yellow.
a procedure to obtain a sample of cells from the bone marrow (aspiration biopsy) or a small core of bone with marrow inside (trephine biopsy).
the technique of using normal red bone marrow to replace cancerous or defective marrow in a patient
a follow-up dose of vaccine, given to reinforce or prolong immunity after an initial course of immunization
a common name for the large and/or small intestines
bleeding within or around the brain that is caused either by injury or by spontaneous rupture of a blood vessel
Techniques that provide pictures of the brain; they are used to detect injury or disease and include X-rays, angiography, CT scanning, MRI, PET scanning, and SPECT.
a stalk of nerve tissue that forms the lowest part of the brain and links with the spinal cord
labored or distressed breathing that includes a change in the rate and depth of breathing or a feeling of breathlessness
a disorder in which the bronchi, the airways connecting the trachea (windpipe) to the lungs, are inflamed
a discolored area under the skin caused by leakage of blood from damaged capillaries (tiny blood vessels)
vocabulary.cn: （皮肤上的）淤伤, 挫伤, 青肿

vocabulary.definition: A stimulant drug found in coffee, tea, cocoa, and cola drinks. Caffeine reduces fatigue, improves concentration, makes the heart pump blood faster, and has a diuretic effect.
the deposition of calcium salts in body tissues that is part of the normal process of bone and teeth formation and the healing of fractures
the body's most abundant mineral, essential for cell function, muscle contraction, the transmission of nerve impulses, and blood clotting
A unit of energy. One calorie is the amount of energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 gram of water by 1°C.
the measurement of the calorie value of foodstuffs or the energy expenditure of a person
a group of diseases characterized by the abnormal and unrestrained growth of cells in body organs or tissues
any of the vessels that carry blood between the smallest arteries, or arterioles, and the smallest veins, or venules
1) an anatomical structure enclosing an organ or body part 2) a gelatinous layer forming the outer surface of some bacterial cells 3) a small soluble case of gelatin containing a dose of medicine
vocabulary.cn: （肾、晶状体或滑液关节等的）被膜, 囊；（细菌细胞外表的）胶质层, 荚膜；（药剂）胶囊

vocabulary.definition: a group of compounds composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, which supply the body with its main source of energy
a colorless, odorless gas
a colorless, odorless, poisonous gas present in motor exhaust fumes and produced by inefficient burning of coal, gas, or oil
any agent capable of causing cancer. Chemicals are the largest group of carcinogens.
the development of a cancer caused by the action of carcinogens (cancer-causing factors) on normal cells
a rare condition caused by an intestinal or lung tumor, called a carcinoid, which secretes excess amounts of the hormone serotonin
any cancerous tumor arising from cells in the covering surface layer or lining membrane of an organ
a halt in the pumping action of the heart that occurs when its rhythmic muscular activity ceases
the study of the function of the heart and the investigation, diagnosis, and medical treatment of disorders of the heart and blood vessels
any disease of the heart muscle that weakens the force of cardiac contractions, thereby reducing the efficiency of blood circulation
the administration of life-saving measures to a person who has suffered a cardiac arrest
pertaining to the heart and blood vessels

a general term for inflammation of any part of the heart or its linings. There are 3 types of carditis: myocarditis (inflammation of the heart muscle), endocarditis (inflammation of the internal lining of the heart), and pericarditis (inflammation of the outer covering of the heart).
an orange pigment found in carrots, tomatoes, and leafy green vegetables

1) a person who is able to pass on an infectious or inherited disease without actually suffering from it 2) a person who possesses a particular gene, especially as a single copy whose effect is masked by a dominant allele 3) a molecule that transfers a specified molecule or ion within the body
vocabulary.cn: 带菌者, 病原携带者; 带隐性基因者; 载体

vocabulary.definition: a type of connective tissue made up of varying amounts of the gel-like substance collagen
a chemical process by which constituents of food stored in the body (for example, fats) are broken down, releasing energy into body cells
vocabulary.cn: 分解; 分解代谢, 异化

vocabulary.definition: an empty space within a solid object, in particular the human body
vocabulary.cn: （尤指人体等固体物的）腔, 洞

vocabulary.definition: the basic structural unit of all living organisms
the processes by which cells multiply, including mitosis and meiosis
a bacterial infection of the skin and the tissues beneath it, usually affecting the face, neck, or legs
the anatomical term for the brain and spinal cord, often abbreviated as CNS
a machine that separates the different components of a body fluid for analysis
a region of the brain behind the brainstem concerned with maintaining posture and balance and coordinating movement
any disease affecting an artery in, and supplying blood to, the brain: for example, atherosclerosis (narrowing of the arteries) or defects or weaknesses in arterial walls causing aneurysm
the largest and most developed part of the brain, the site of most conscious and intelligent activities
another term for a pharmacist
the term that usually describes the use of drugs to treat cancer but which may also describe the use of antibiotics to treat infectious diseases
the upper part of the trunk. Known technically as the thorax, the chest extends from the base of the neck down to the diaphragm.
pain in the chest, which is often without serious cause, but which may be a symptom of an underlying disorder requiring urgent treatment
one of the most frequently performed medical tests, usually carried out to examine the heart or lungs to confirm diagnoses of heart disorders and lung diseases
a complementary treatment for a range of disorders, including back pain, based on manipulation of the spine
partial or complete inability to breathe due to an obstruction of the airways
a fat-like substance that is an important constituent of body cells and is also involved in the formation of hormones and bile salts
a rare genetic bleeding disorder in which there is deficient production of one of the proteins in blood needed for blood coagulation
vocabulary.cn: 基氏症, 乙型血友病

vocabulary.definition: thread-like structures in the nuclei of cells. Chromosomes carry inherited information in the form of genes, which govern all cell activity and function.
variations from normal in the number or structure of chromosomes contained in a person's cells
study of the chromosomes in body cells to discover whether a chromosomal abnormality is present or to establish its nature
a term describing a disorder or set of symptoms that has persisted for a long time
any biological pattern based on a cycle approximately 24 hours long, also called a diurnal rhythm
the heart and blood vessels, which together maintain a continuous flow of blood throughout the body

a condition of the liver arising from long-term damage to its cells. In cirrhosis, bands of fibrosis (internal scarring) develop, leaving nodules of regenerating cells that are inadequately supplied with blood.
a cramp-like pain in a muscle, most often in the legs, due to inadequate blood supply
vocabulary.cn: （尤指动脉阻塞引起的）间歇性跛行

vocabulary.definition: intense fear of being in enclosed spaces, such as lifts, or of being in crowded areas
an exact copy. In medicine, the term usually refers to copies of cells, genes, or organisms. Clones of cells are all descended from one original cell.
vocabulary.cn: 克隆；无性繁殖的生物, 克隆生物（细胞）

vocabulary.definition: the spiral-shaped organ situated in the labyrinth of the inner ear that transforms sound vibrations into nerve impulses for transmission to the brain, enabling hearing.
an oil obtained from the liver of fresh cod, which is a valuable source of vitamin A and vitamin D.
inflammation of the colon causing diarrhea, usually with blood and mucus
a tough, fibrous protein. Collagen is the body's major structural protein, forming an important part of tendons, bones, and connective tissue.
the common name for the clavicle
the major part of the large intestine
examination of the inside of the colon by means of a flexible, fiber-optic viewing instrument called a colonoscope
any abnormality in color vision that causes difficulty distinguishing between certain colors (also called color blindness)
a state of unconsciousness and unresponsiveness to external stimuli (for example, pinching) or internal stimuli (such as a full bladder)
A group of therapies, often described as 'alternative', which are now increasingly used to complement or to act as an alternative to conventional medicine.

1) a group or combination of related signs and symptoms that form a syndrome 2) a collection of substances of similar structure or function 3) In psychology, a complex is a group of unconscious ideas and memories that have emotional importance.
vocabulary.cn: 症候群; 复合物; 合成物; 聚合体; 情结；变态心理

vocabulary.definition: a condition resulting from a preceding disorder or from its treatment
brief unconsciousness due to disturbance of the electrical activity in the brain following a violent blow to the head or neck
vocabulary.cn: （头部被击导致的）暂时知觉丧失; 脑震荡

vocabulary.definition: the formation of a specific response to a specific stimulus
the ethical principle that a doctor does not disclose information given in confidence by a patient
present at birth. Congenital abnormalities (sometimes called birth defects) are either inherited or result from damage or infection occurring in the uterus or at the time of birth.
the transparent membrane covering the sclera (white of the eye) and lining the inside of the eyelids
inflammation of the conjunctiva, causing redness, discomfort, and discharge from the affected eye
a state of alertness in which a person is fully aware of his or her thoughts, surroundings, and intentions
the legal term describing a patient's agreement to a doctor performing an operation, arranging drug treatment, or carrying out diagnostic tests
vocabulary.cn: 同意, 赞成, 准许

vocabulary.definition: a term used to describe a disease that can be transferred from person to person by ordinary social contact
vocabulary.cn: （疾病）接触传染（性）的, 触染（性）的

vocabulary.definition: a substance opaque to X-rays, introduced into hollow or fluid-filled body parts to show them up on X-ray film
one of a number of drugs subject to restricted use because of their potential for abuse
any structure that encircles like a crown. The term usually refers to the coronary arteries.
the outer layer of certain organs, such as the brain or kidneys
vocabulary.cn: 脑皮层；（肾、小脑或头发等的）皮层; 皮质

vocabulary.definition: an operation performed to improve appearance rather than to cure or treat disease
advice and psychological support from health professionals to help people deal with personal difficulties
a painful spasm in a muscle caused by excessive and prolonged contraction of the muscle fibers
twelve pairs of nerves that emerge directly from the underside of the brain
the part of the skull around the brain
a protein produced in the body in response to inflammation
a term used to mean seriously ill or to describe a crucial state of illness from which a patient may not recover
vocabulary.cn: 病情严重的; 病危的

vocabulary.definition: a chronic inflammatory disease affecting the gastrointestinal tract
vocabulary.cn: 克罗恩病，节段性肠炎（引起大肠直肠多部位长年疼痛）

vocabulary.definition: a procedure to determine compatibility between the blood of a person requiring a blood transfusion and that of a donor
a prefix meaning ice cold, used medically to indicate that a procedure uses freezing or low temperatures
the preservation of living cells by freezing
vocabulary.cn: 深低温保存，也称为超低温保存

vocabulary.definition: a diagnostic technique in which the combined use of a computer and X-rays passed through the body at different angles produces cross-sectional images of tissues
a growth of bacteria or other microorganisms, cells, or tissues cultivated artificially in the laboratory
an ancient form of treatment, still used in folk healing in some countries, which draws blood to the surface by applying a small heated vessel to the skin
a suffix that denotes a cell. For example, a leukocyte is a white blood cell
a prefix that means related to a cell, as in cytology, the study of cells
a protein released by body cells in response to the presence of disease-causing organisms such as viruses
vocabulary.cn: 细胞因子, 细胞活素

vocabulary.definition: the study of individual cells

a harmless condition in which dead skin is shed from the scalp, often producing white flakes. The usual cause is the rash seborrhoeic dermatitis. Frequent use of an antidandruff shampoo usually controls the dandruff.

surgical removal of foreign material and/or dead, damaged, or infected tissue from a wound or burn in order to expose healthy tissue. Such treatment promotes the healthy healing of badly damaged skin, muscle, and other tissues in the body.
the dissolving of minerals in a tooth. Dental decalcification is the first stage of tooth decay.
a hazard of divers and of others who work in or breathe compressed air or other gas mixtures.
vocabulary.cn: 减压病（一种由于气压突然下降引起的疾病）

vocabulary.definition: techniques used by the mind to cope with unpleasant or unwelcome emotions, impulses, experiences, or events. Repression of emotions surrounding a particular event or refusing to accept an event (denial) are both defence mechanisms.

administration of one or more brief electric shocks to the heart, usually via 2 metal plates, or paddles, placed on the chest over the heart. It is performed to return a heart's rhythm to normal in some types of arrhythmia (irregular or rapid heartbeat), such as atrial fibrillation or ventricular fibrillation.
any malformation or distortion of part of the body. Deformities may be congenital (present from birth), or they may be acquired as a result of injury, disorder, or disuse.

physical and/or chemical changes in cells, tissues, or organs that reduce their efficiency. It is a feature of aging and may also be due to a disease process. Other known causes include injury, reduced blood supply, poisoning, or a diet deficient in specific vitamin.

a term covering a range of conditions in which there is progressive impairment of the structure and function of part of the body. The definition excludes conditions due to inflammation, infection, altered immune responses, chemical or physical damage, or cancerous change.
a condition in which a person's water content is at a dangerously low level.
behaviour in a young person that would be considered a crime in an adult. The term is often extended to include noncriminal behaviour such as drug abuse, playing truant, or running away from home.
vocabulary.cn: （尤指青少年的）犯罪，不良行为

vocabulary.definition: a state of acute mental confusion, commonly brought on by physical illness.
a fixed, irrational idea not shared by others and not responding to reasoned argument. The idea in a paranoid delusion involves persecution or jealousy.
a condition characterised by a deterioration in brain function. Dementia is almost always due to Alzheimer's disease or to cerebrovascular disease, including strokes.
a tropical disease caused by a virus spread by the mosquito AEDES AEGYPTI.
an imaging technique that uses low-dose X-rays to measure bone density, as determined by the concentration of calcified material.
the science or profession concerned with the teeth and their supporting structures.
a substance that removes unpleasant odours, especially body odours.
inflammation of the skin, sometimes due to an allergy. Dermatitis is the same as eczema, and the terms can be used interchangeably.
the branch of medicine concerned with the skin and its disorders.
the inner layer of the skin.

the process of finding the nature of a disorder. The doctor listens to a patient's account of his or her illness and a physical examination is usually involved. Tests may be ordered after the formation of a provisional diagnosis.
a filtering technique used to remove waste products from the blood and excess fluid from the body as a treatment for kidney failure.

a synthetic, opioid analgesic drug similar to morphine; it is another name for heroin. Diamorphine is used to relieve severe pain and also to relieve distress in acute heart failure. It carries the risk of dependence so is prescribed with caution.
the shaft, or central portion, of a long bone, such as the femur.
(also diarrhea) increased fluidity, frequency, or volume of bowel movements. It may be acute or chronic.
the period in the heartbeat cycle when the heart muscle is at rest; it alternates with systole, the period of muscular contraction.
the application of nutritional science to maintain or restore health.
the process by which food is broken down into smaller components that can be transported and used by the body.
the group of organs responsible for digestion.
a condition in which a body cavity, tube, or opening is enlarged or stretched due to normal physiological processes or because of disease.
an instrument for stretching and enlarging a narrowed body cavity, tube, or opening.
a visible emission of fluid from an orifice or a break in the skin.
a flat, circular, plate-like structure containing cartilage that lines the joints between adjacent vertebrae (bones) in the spine.

a common disorder of the spine, in which an intervertebral disc ruptures and part of its pulpy core protrudes. It causes painful and at times disabling pressure on a nerve root or, less commonly, on the spinal cord.
substances that kill micro organisms and thus prevent infection.
complete displacement of the 2 bones in a joint so that they are no longer in contact, usually as a result of injury.
cutting of body tissues during surgery or for the purposes of anatomical study.
drugs that help remove excess water from the body by increasing the amount lost as urine.
a sensation of unsteadiness and light-headedness.
a person who provides blood for transfusion, tissues or organs for transplantation, eggs, or semen for artificial insemination.
a neurotransmitter found in the brain and around some blood vessels.
a term used to refer to the amount of a drug taken at a particular time, or to the amount of radiation an individual is exposed to during a session of radio therapy.

a type of controlled trial that tests the effectiveness of a treatment or compares the benefits of different treatments. In double-blind trials, neither the patients nor the doctors assessing the treatments know which patients are receiving which treatment.
a chromosomal abnormality resulting in a variable degree of learning difficulties and a characteristic physical appearance.
an outmoded term for generalized oedema (fluid collection in body tissues). It is not a disease in itself, but a sign of disease, especially congestive heart failure or kidney disease.
a tube or a tube-like passage leading from a gland to allow the flow of fluids, for example, the tear ducts.
inflammation of the duodenum (the first part of the small intestine), often causing abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
the first part of the small intestine extending from the pylorus to the ligament of Treitz, which marks the boundary between the duodenum and the jejunum.
a prefix meaning abnormal, difficult, painful, or faulty, as in dysuria (pain on passing urine).
a reading disability characterized by difficulty in coping with written symbols.
a disturbance in the ability to select the words with which to speak and write and/or to understand speech or writing.
any abnormality of growth. The term applies to deformities in structures such as the skull and to abnormalities of single cells.
any disorder in which the structure and normal activity of cells within a tissue have been disrupted by inadequate nutrition.
the circular membrane that separates the outer ear from the middle ear. The eardrum vibrates in response to sound waves, conducting the sound to the inner ear through the ossicles.
a yellow or brown secretion, also called cerumen, produced by glands in the outer ear canal.
a dangerous and highly contagious viral infection that causes severe haemorrhaging from the skin and mucous membranes.
vocabulary.cn: 埃博拉热病（致命出血热，由病毒引起）

vocabulary.definition: a method of obtaining an image of the structure and movement of the heart with ultrasound.
a suffix that denotes surgical removal. For example, tonsillectomy is surgical removal of the tonsils.
a term used to describe a body structure that occurs in an abnormal location or position, or a body function that occurs at an abnormal time.
An inflammation of the skin, usually causing itching and sometimes scaling or blisters.
the process by which fluid escapes. The term also describes an abnormal collection of fluid, such as blood, pus, or plasma, in the tissues or a body cavity.
the hinge joint formed where the lower end of the humerus meets the upper ends of the radius and ulna.
the unborn child during the first 8 weeks of its development following conception; for the rest of the pregnancy it is known as a fetus.
the study of the development of the embryo and then the fetus from conception until birth.
a disease in which the walls of the air sacs in the lungs, known as alveoli, are progressively destroyed, thus reducing the area of lung available for exchange of gases.
inflammation of the brain, and sometimes also the meninges, usually due to a viral infection.
inflammation of the brain and spinal cord, causing damage to the nervous system.
any disorder affecting the brain, especially chronic degenerative conditions.
a term applied to a disease or disorder that is constantly present in a particular region or in a specific group of people.
a gland that secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream rather than through a duct.
the study of the endocrine system, including the investigation and treatment of its disorders.
of a disease or disorder that arises within the body rather than being caused by external factors.
a tube-like viewing instrument that is inserted into a body cavity to investigate or treat disorders.
examination of a body cavity for diagnosis or treatment by means of an endoscope.
vocabulary.cn: 内窥镜检查；内视镜检查法

vocabulary.definition: a procedure in which fluid is passed into the rectum through a tube inserted into the anus.
inflammation of the small intestine.
a protein that regulates the rate of a chemical reaction in the body.
a term applied to a disease that for most of the time is rare in a community but suddenly spreads rapidly to affect a large number of people.
the branch of medicine concerned with the occurrence and distribution of disease, including infectious diseases and noninfectious diseases such as cancer.
a tendency to have recurrent seizures.
an antibiotic drug used to treat infections of the skin, chest, throat, and ears.
a colourless liquid that produces unconsciousness when inhaled. Ether was the first general anaesthetic.
the use of medical knowledge to end a person's life painlessly in order to relieve suffering.
surgical cutting out of diseased tissue, such as a breast lump, from surrounding healthy tissue.
injury to the surface of the skin or a mucous membrane caused by physical abrasion, such as scratching.
discharge of waste material from the body, including the by-products of digestion, waste products from the repair of tissues, and excess water.
a gland that secretes substances through a duct on to the inner surface of an organ or the outer surface of the body.
of a disease or disorder, having a cause that is external to the body, such as infection.
a poison released by certain types of bacteria that enters the bloodstream and causes widespread effects around the body.
cough remedies that encourage the coughing up of sputum.
the coughing up and spitting out of sputum.
the discharge of fluid from blood vessels into surrounding tissue. In most cases, exudation is due to inflammation.
a common term for aching or discomfort in or around the eye.
the 7th cranial nerve, which arises from structures in the brainstem and sends branches to the face, neck, salivary glands, and outer ear.
a group of disorders in which a patient's symptoms mimic those of a true illness but which have been invented by, and are controlled by, the patient.
a temperature scale in which the melting point of ice is 32º and the boiling point of water is 212º.
a term applied to a characteristic or disorder that runs in families.
spontaneous, irregular, and usually continual contractions of a muscle that is apparently at rest.
vocabulary.cn: 自发性收缩（指肌肉、神经等）

vocabulary.definition: inflammation of a layer of fascia (fibrous connective tissue), causing pain and tenderness.
organic acids, containing carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, that are constituents of fats and oils.
a major blood vessel that supplies oxygenated blood to the leg.
the medical name for the thighbone, the longest bone in the body.
a complex of iron and protein, found mainly in the liver and spleen, which is the principal form of iron storage in the body.
the ability to produce children without undue difficulty.
the unborn child from the end of the 8th week after conception until birth. For the first 8 weeks, the unborn child is called an embryo.
a substance that is produced in the blood during the process of blood clotting.
a protein that is present in blood and which is converted into fibrin during the blood clotting process.
the breakdown of fibrin, the principal component of any blood clot.
an overgrowth of scar tissue or connective tissue. Fibrous tissue may be formed as an exaggerated healing response to infection, inflammation, or injury.
the process of replacing a chipped or decayed area of tooth with an inactive material.
excessive accumulation of fluid in body tissues.
a harmless orange dye used in ophthalmology as an aid to the diagnosis of certain eye disorders.
a mineral that helps to prevent dental caries by strengthening tooth enamel, making it more resistant to acid attacks.
reddening of the face, and sometimes the neck, caused by dilation of the blood vessels near the skin surface.
a vitamin that is essential for the production of red blood cells by the bone marrow. Folic acid is contained in a variety of foods, particularly liver and raw vegetables.
inflammation of 1 or more hair follicles as a result of a staphylococcal infection.
any substance added to food for the purposes of preservation or to improve its acceptability in terms of taste, colour, or consistency.
a tweezer-like instrument used for handling tissues or equipment during surgical procedures.
the branch of medicine concerned with the law, especially criminal law.

a colourless, pungent, irritant gas. In medicine, a solution of formaldehyde and a small amount of alcohol in water, a preparation known as formalin, is used to preserve tissue specimens or to harden them before they are stained and examined.
a way of expressing the constituents of a chemical in symbols and numbers.
a break in a bone, usually across its width. There are 2 main types: closed (simple) or open (compound) fractures.
a tiny patch of pigmentation that occurs on sun-exposed skin.
molecules that bind to and destroy body cells. Free radicals can derive from external sources such as smoke, sunlight, and food, but they are mostly produced in the body following chemical reactions.
a term referring to the front part of an organ (for example, the frontal lobe of the brain).
damage to tissues caused by extremely cold temperatures.
a method of preparing a biopsy specimen that provides a rapid indication of whether or not a tissue, such as a breast lump, is cancerous.
stiffness and pain in the shoulder that makes normal movement of the joint impossible. In severe cases, the shoulder may be completely rigid, and pain may be intense.
diseases that are caused by the multiplication and spread of fungi.
simple parasitic life-forms that include mushrooms, toadstools, yeasts, and moulds. Disease causing fungi can be divided into 2 groups: filamentous fungi and yeasts.
a term describing the ability to kill fungi.

a small, pear-shaped sac situated under the liver that stores bile. Bile, produced by the liver, passes into the gallbladder via the hepatic and cystic ducts. It is released into the intestine via the common bile duct.
lumps of solid matter found in the gallbladder, or in the bile ducts. Gallstones are composed mainly of cholesterol and bile pigments from the breakdown of red blood cells.
a group of nerve cells that have a common function; for example, the basal ganglia in the brain are concerned with the control of muscular movements.
removal of the stomach (total gastrectomy) or, more commonly, part of the stomach (partial gastrectomy).
a hormone produced by cells in the stomach lining. Gastrin causes the stomach to produce more acid and helps to propel food through the digestive tract.
inflammation of the stomach lining. This may be acute or chronic.
inflammation of the stomach and intestines, usually causing sudden upsets that last for 2 or 3 days.
the study of the digestive system and the diseases and disorders affecting it.
examination of the stomach using a type of endoscope inserted through the mouth.
a thin, transparent fabric used for dressings and surgical sponges.
the study of inheritance, the chemical basis by which characteristics are determined, and the causes of the similarities and differences among individuals of a species or between different species.
the complete set of human genetic material. The human genome consists of 23 chromosomes, which, together, contain about 30,000 genes.
the popular term used to describe any microorganisms that cause disease, such as viruses and bacteria.
a group of specialized cells that manufacture and release chemical substances, such as hormones and enzymes, into the body. There are two main types of glands: endocrine and exocrine.

a condition in which the pressure of the fluid in the eye is abnormally high, causing the compression and obstruction of the small blood vessels that nourish the retina. This may result in nerve fibre destruction and gradual loss of vision.
a type of brain tumour arising from the supporting connective tissue, (glial cells), in the brain.
any of a group of proteins that are insoluble in water but soluble in dilute salt solutions.
a filtering unit of the kidney that consists of a cluster of capillaries enclosed in a capsule and supplied with blood from the renal artery.

a simple sugar that is naturally present in fruits and is a product of the digestion of starch and sucrose. It is the chief source of energy for the body and is carried to all tissues in the blood. The term blood sugar refers to glucose in the bloodstream.
a combination of gliadins and glutenins (types of proteins) formed when certain cereal flours (notably wheat flour) are mixed with water.
a colourless syrupy liquid that has a sweet taste. Glycerol is prepared from fats and oils; it is an essential constituent of triglycerides (simple fats).
the main form of carbohydrate stored in the body, found mainly in the liver and in muscles.
a common metabolic disorder that causes attacks of arthritis, usually in a single joint (most commonly the base of the big toe).
the hollow between the lower abdomen and top of the thigh.
the soft tissue surrounding the teeth that protects underlying structures and keeps the teeth in position in the jaw.
(also gynecology) the medical specialty concerned with the female reproductive tract.
the study of blood and its formation, as well as the investigation and treatment of disorders that affect the blood and the bone marrow.
blood in the urine, which may or may not be visible to the naked eye. In small amounts, it may give the urine a smoky appearance.
one of the 2 means of dialysis used to treat kidney failure.
the oxygen-carrying pigment that is present in red blood cells.
an inherited bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of a blood protein, factor VIII, which is essential for blood clotting.
the medical term for bleeding.
a perception that occurs when there is no external stimulus.
the extent to which a physical or mental disability interferes with normal functioning and causes the person to be disadvantaged.
a burning pain in the centre of the chest, which may travel from the tip of the breastbone to the throat. It may be caused by eating rich or spicy food, or by drinking alcohol.
a structure at the exit of a heart chamber that allows blood to flow out of the chamber, but prevents backwash.
a life-threatening condition in which overexposure to heat coupled with a breakdown of the body's heat regulating mechanisms cause the body to become dangerously overheated.
paralysis or weakness on 1 side of the body, caused by damage or disease affecting the motor nerve tracts in the opposite side of the brain.
surgical removal of the liver.
a serious disease of the liver. There are three main forms: hepatitis A (the least serious, caused by infected food), hepatitis B and hepatitis C (both very serious and caused by infected blood)
a type of liver cancer.
the transmission of traits and disorders through genetic mechanisms.
a measure of the extent to which a disease or disorder is the result of inherited factors, as opposed to environmental influences such as diet and climate.
a narcotic drug similar to morphine. When used for medical purposes, it is generally known as diamorphine.
any of a variety of conditions characterized by an eruption of small, usually painful, blisters on the skin.
a sudden, involuntary contraction of the diaphragm followed by rapid closure of the vocal cords.
the joint between the pelvis and the upper end of the femur.
a structure in the limbic system of the brain.
the study of tissues, including their cellular structure and function. The main application of histology in medicine is in the diagnosis of disease.
a branch of histology concerned with the effects of disease on the microscopic structure of tissues.

a rough, husky, or croaking voice. Short-lived hoarseness is often due to overuse of the voice, which strains the muscles in the larynx. It is also commonly caused by inflammation of the vocal cords in acute laryngitis.
a form of therapy that treats the whole person, not just specific disease symptoms. A holistic approach is emphasized by many practitioners of complementary medicine.

a system of complementary medicine. Homeopathy involves administering minute doses of medicines which, in a healthy person, would be capable of inducing symptoms of the condition that is being treated.
the automatic processes by which the body maintains a constant internal environment despite external changes.
a chemical released into the bloodstream by a gland or tissue that has a specific effect on tissues elsewhere in the body.
a hospital or part of a hospital devoted to the care of patients who are terminally ill.
the bone of the upper arm. The dome-shaped head of the bone lies at an angle to the shaft and fits into a socket in the scapula to form the shoulder joint.
a soft, painless swelling in the scrotum caused by the space around a testis filling with fluid.
a strong acid released by the stomach lining. This acid forms part of the stomach juices and helps to digest proteins.
the science and practice of preserving health. The word hygiene is commonly equated with cleanliness, but it can also refer to public health.
a prefix meaning above, excessive, or greater than normal.
an abnormally high level of glucose in the blood that occurs in people with untreated or inadequately controlled diabetes mellitus.
enlargement of an organ or tissue due to an increase in the number of its cells.
persistently raised blood pressure exceeding about 140 mmHg (systolic) and 90 mmHg (diastolic) at rest.
enlargement of an organ or tissue due to an increase in the size, rather than number, of its constituent cells.
a trance-like state of altered awareness characterized by extreme suggestibility.
a prefix meaning under, below, or less than normal.
an abnormally low level of glucose in the blood.
the medical term for low blood pressure. In its most common form, known as postural hypotension, symptoms occur after abruptly standing or sitting up.
a region of the brain, roughly the size of a cherry, situated behind the eyes and the thalamus.
a fall in body temperature to below 35°C. Most cases occur in sick, elderly people exposed to low temperatures.
a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used as a painkiller to treat conditions such as headache, menstrual pain, and injury to soft tissues (such as muscles and ligaments).
of unknown cause. For example, epilepsy with no apparent cause is called idiopathic epilepsy.
reduced physical activity, for example, through disease, injury, or following major surgery.
the body's defensive reaction to microorganisms, cancer cells, transplanted tissue, and other substances or materials that are recognized as antigenic or 'foreign'.
a collection of cells and proteins that works to protect the body from harmful microorganisms, such as bacteria, viruses, and fungi.
a state of protection against disease through the activities of the immune system.
the process of inducing immunity as a preventive measure against infectious diseases.
disorders in which there is a failure of the immune system's defences to fight infection and tumours.
a type of protein found in blood and tissue fluids, also known as an antibody.
the discipline concerned with the immune system. Immunologists study the immune system's functioning and investigate and treat immune system disorders.
stimulation of the immune system as a treatment for cancer.
any material, either natural or artificial, inserted into the body for medical or cosmetic purposes.
one of the 2 principal measures (the other is prevalence) of how common a disease is in a defined population.
a cut made into the tissues of the body by a scalpel (surgical knife).
the time during which an infectious disease develops, from the point when the infecting organism enters the body until symptoms appear.
a common term (known medically as dyspepsia) covering a variety of symptoms brought on by eating, including heartburn, abdominal pain, nausea, and flatulence.
the establishment in the body of disease-causing micro organisms (such as bacteria, viruses, or fungi)
any illness caused by a specific microorganism
a neurotic state of mind that develops because of repeated hurts or failures in the past
an exaggerated opinion of one's own importance or abilities
the inability to produce offspring, which may result from a problem in either the male or the female reproductive system, or, in many cases, from a combination of problems in both
the presence of animal parasites (such as mites, ticks, or lice) in the skin or hair, or of worms (such as tapeworms) inside the body
build-up of substances or cells within a tissue that are either not normally found in it or are usually present only in smaller amounts
redness, swelling, heat, and pain in a tissue due to injury or infection

a viral infection of the respiratory tract (air passages), typically causing fever, headache, muscle ache, and weakness. Popularly known as 'flu', it is spread by infected droplets from coughs or sneezes.

The act of taking any substance (for example, food, drink, or medications) into the body through the mouth. The term also refers to the process by which certain cells (for example, some white blood cells) surround and then engulf small particles.
The act of taking in breath. An inhalation is also a substance, in the form of a gas, vapour, powder, or aerosol, to be breathed in.
a device used for administering a drug in powder or vapour form, used mainly in the treatment of various respiratory disorders, including asthma and chronic bronchitis.
The transmission of characteristics and disorders from parents to their children through the influence of genes.
The process of preventing any mental or physical activity.
Introduction of a substance into the body from a syringe via a needle.
Harm to any part of the body.

The act of introducing a small quantity of a foreign substance into the body, usually by injection, for the purpose of stimulating the immune system to produce antibodies (protective proteins) against the substance.
A term used to refer to any of the large group of substances that do not contain carbon and to a few simple carbon compounds (for example, carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide).
Care or therapy in hospital following admission.
A term for serious mental disorder.
A Latin term meaning 'in place'. The phrase 'carcinoma in situ' is used to describe tissue (particularly of the skin or cervix) that is cancerous only in its surface cells.
vocabulary.cn: 原位：限于原来部位，而未侵犯邻近组织

vocabulary.definition: Difficulty in falling asleep or in staying asleep.
An innate primitive urge.
A hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates glucose levels in the blood.
The ability to understand concepts and to reason them out.
Constant close monitoring and treatment of seriously ill patients that enables treatment to be tailored to the patient's condition on an hour-by-hour basis.
The major part of the digestive tract, extending from the exit of the stomach to the anus.
A general term for a condition resulting from poisoning.
a prefix that means within, as in the term intramuscular (within a muscle).
a term to describe any condition that does not respond to treatment.
a term meaning within a vein, as in intravenous infusion (slow introduction of a substance into a vein) and intravenous injection (rapid introduction of a substance into a vein).
a person more concerned with his or her inner world. Introverts prefer to work alone, are shy, quiet, and withdrawn when under stress.
tending to spread throughout body tissues; the term is usually applied to cancerous tumours or harmful microorganisms.
The performance of biological processes in a laboratory rather than within the body. The term in vitro literally means 'in glass'.
vocabulary.cn: 在生物体外进行的; 在试管内的

vocabulary.definition: A particle that carries an electrical charge; positive ions are called cations and negative ions are called anions.
The abbreviation for intelligence quotient, an age-related measure of intelligence.
The colored part of the eye, made up of a loose framework of transparent collagen and muscle fibers, that lies behind the cornea and in front of the lens.
A mineral essential for the formation of certain enzymes, haemoglobin (the oxygen-carrying pigment in red blood cells), and myoglobin (the oxygen-carrying pigment in muscle cells).
Nursing procedures (also called barrier nursing) designed to prevent a patient from infecting others or from being infected.
An intense irritation or tickling sensation in the skin.
A suffix meaning 'inflammation of'.
The mobile bone of the face, also known as the mandible.
Fatigue and interruption of the sleep–wake cycle caused by disturbance of normal body biorhythms as a result of flying across different time zones.
The junction between 2 or more bones.
either of a pair of organs responsible for filtering blood and producing urine.
An operation in which a person with chronic kidney failure receives a healthy kidney, either from a living donor or a cadaver.
The hinge joint between the femur (thigh bone) and tibia (shin).
Surgery to replace a diseased knee joint with an artificial substitute, usually a metal or plastic implant.
The collective term for the convoluted structures of the inner ear.
Inflammation of the labyrinth.
One of the sugars present in milk; a disaccharide carbohydrate.
The organ in the throat responsible for voice production, commonly called the voice-box.
A device that produces a concentrated beam of light radiation; 'laser' is an acronym for 'light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation'.
Use of a laser beam in a variety of medical procedures. High-intensity laser beams cut through tissue and cause blood clotting.
Relating to, or situated on, one side.
The internal optical component of the eye responsible for focusing; also called the crystalline lens.
A protein that has a role in the regulation of fat storage by the body.
Any of several types of cancer in which there is a disorganized proliferation of white blood cells within the bone marrow.
The number of years a person can expect to live.
The surgical process of tying off a duct or a blood vessel with a ligature in order to stop bleeding.
An abnormal pattern of walking in which the movements of one leg (or of the hip on one side of the body) are different from those of the other.
One of 2 fleshy folds around the entrance to the mouth.
A way of understanding speech by interpreting movements of the mouth and tongue. Lip reading is often used by people who are deaf.
Surgical removal of a calculus (stone) from part of the urinary tract.
The largest organ of the body, this roughly wedge-shaped, red-brown structure lies in the upper right abdominal cavity, directly below the diaphragm.
One of the 2 main organs of the respiratory system.
A dietary system in which foods with a balance of yin and yang are eaten.
A cell in the immune system.
An element essential in the diet for the formation of bones and teeth, muscle contraction, nerve impulse transmission, and activation of many enzymes.
Failure to adapt to a change in one's environment, resulting in inability to cope with work or social activities.
A serious disease caused by parasitic protozoa called plasmodia.
A deformity, particularly one resulting from faulty development.
A condition that tends to become progressively worse and to result in death.
A mental disorder characterized by episodes of overactivity, elation, or irritability.
A therapeutic technique involving the skillful use of the hands to move a part of the body, joint, or muscle to treat certain disorders.
A skin test for tuberculosis.
vocabulary.cn: 结核菌素皮肤试验，曼托试验

vocabulary.definition: Rubbing and kneading areas of the body, usually with the hands. Massage increases the blood flow and relaxes muscles; it may be used to relieve muscle spasm, treat muscle injury, and reduce oedema.
A potentially dangerous viral illness that causes fever and a characteristic rash.
Concentrating on an object, a word, or an idea with the aim of inducing an altered state of consciousness.
A prefix meaning very large, as in megacolon, a condition in which the colon is greatly enlarged. The prefix megalo- has the same meaning.
A layer of tissue that covers or lines a body surface or forms a barrier.
A measurement of the intellectual development of a person, with regard to the normal age at which that level of achievement is attained.
Impaired intellectual development, also known as general learning difficulties or disability.
A group of disorders in which some aspect of body chemistry is disturbed.
A collective term for all the chemical processes that take place in the body.
A popular term for a microorganism, particularly a harmful type that causes disease.
The study of microorganisms, particularly those that are pathogenic or disease-causing.
A tiny, single-celled living organism. Most microorganisms are too small to be seen by the naked eye.
An instrument for producing a magnified image of a small object. Microscopes are used to examine the structure and chemical composition of cells and tissues.
Surgery in which the surgeon views the operation site via a special binocular microscope with pedal-operated magnification, focusing, and movement.
A severe headache, typically lasting 4–72 hours, accompanied by visual disturbances and/or nausea and vomiting.
In nutrition, chemical elements that are essential in the diet.
Dietary supplements containing one or more minerals in tablet or liquid form.
The smallest complete unit of a substance that can exist independently and still retain the characteristic properties of that substance.
To maintain a constant watch on the condition of a patient. Also, any device used to carry out monitoring.
One of the main types of white blood cell.
The state or condition of being diseased.
An opioid analgesic drug derived from the opium poppy.
The death rate, which is the number of deaths per 100,000 (or 10,000 or 1,000) of the population per year.
A term used to describe anything that brings about movement, such as a muscle or a nerve.
Any of a large group of fungi that exist as many-celled, filamentous colonies.
An open sore caused by a break in the mucous membrane lining the mouth.
A form of treatment, often used in conjunction with acupuncture, in which a cone of wormwood leaves (moxa) or certain other plant materials is burned just above the skin to relieve internal pain.
The abbreviation for magnetic resonance imaging.
A structure composed of bundles of specialized cells capable of contraction and relaxation to create movement.
A change in a cell's DNA.
A hard, curved plate on the fingers and toes composed of keratin (a tough protein).
The passage connecting the nasal cavity behind the nose to the top of the throat behind the soft palate.
The sensation of needing to vomit.
A popular term for the umbilicus, the depression in the abdomen that marks the point at which the umbilical cord was attached to the fetus.
The part of the body that supports the head and serves as a passageway between the head and brain and the body.
The death of tissue cells.
A bundle of nerve fibres which travel to a common location.
The body system that gathers and stores information and is in overall control of the body.
Pain caused by irritation of, or damage to, a nerve.
A term that literally means inflammation of a nerve.
The medical discipline concerned with the study of the nervous system and its disorders.
The term used to describe a nerve cell.
The branch of pathology that is concerned with the causes and effects of disorders of the nervous system.
A drug in tobacco which acts as a stimulant and is responsible for dependence on tobacco.
The inability to see well in dim light.
A colourless, odourless gas that makes up 78 per cent of the Earth's atmosphere.
A colourless gas, sometimes called laughing gas.
A small, rounded mass of tissue.
A small lump of tissue.
Substances found in all living matter that have a fundamental role in the propagation of life.
The central core, structure, or focal point of an object.
Loss of sensation in part of the body caused by interference with the passage of impulses along sensory nerves.
An essential dietary factor, including carbohydrates, proteins, certain fats, vitamins, and minerals.
The scientific study of food and the processes by which it is digested and assimilated.
A condition in which excess fat has accumulated in the body.
A psychiatric condition in which a person is dogged by persistent ideas (obsessions) that lead to repetitive, ritualized acts (compulsions).
The branch of medicine concerned with pregnancy and antenatal care, childbirth, and postnatal care.
Blockage of a passage, canal, opening, or vessel in the body.
A branch of medicine dealing with the effects of various occupations on health, and with an individual's capacity for particular types of work.
The study of the causes, development, characteristics, and treatment of tumours, particularly cancers.
A suffix that denotes a disease or disorder, as in neuropathy (a disorder of the peripheral nerves).
A term applied to a condition that is suitable for surgical treatment, such as an accessible noncancerous tumor.
A specialized hospital room in which surgical procedures are performed.
The study of the eye and the diagnosis and treatment of the disorders that affect it.
An instrument used to examine the inside of the eye.
A substance obtained from the unripe seed pods of the poppy plant.
A person who fits and sells glasses or contact lenses.
vocabulary.cn: 眼镜商、验光师、配制眼镜技师

vocabulary.definition: The practice of assessing vision to establish whether glasses or contact lenses are needed to correct a visual defect, as carried out by an optometrist.
Measures to keep the mouth and teeth clean and reduce the risk of tooth decay, gingivitis and other gum disorders, and halitosis.
The socket in the skull containing the eyeball, protective fat, blood vessels, muscles, and nerves.
A collection of various tissues integrated into a distinct structural unit to perform specific functions.
Related to a body organ; having organs or an organized structure; or related to organisms or to substances from them.
A general term for an individual animal or plant.
The branch of surgery concerned with disorders of the bones and joints and their associated muscles, tendons, and ligaments.
A prefix denoting a relationship to bone, as in osteoporosis, a condition in which the bones thin and weaken.
A common joint disease characterized by degeneration of the cartilage that lines joints or by formation of osteophytes, leading to pain, stiffness, and occasionally loss of function.
A prefix that denotes a relationship to the ear, as in otorrhoea (discharge from the ear).
vocabulary.cn: 表示'与耳朵相关'的前缀

vocabulary.definition: Medical care on a same-day basis in a hospital or clinic.
A colourless, odourless gas that makes up 21 per cent of the Earth's atmosphere.
A rare form of oxygen, ozone is a poisonous, faintly blue gas that is produced by the action of electrical discharges (such as lightning) on oxygen molecules.
A small device that supplies electrical impulses to the heart to maintain a regular heartbeat.
The branch of medicine that is concerned with the development of children, and the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of childhood diseases.
The treatment of pain, usually with analgesic drugs.
A claimed remedy for all diseases. No such remedy is known.
A tapered gland that lies across the back of the abdomen, behind the stomach.
A medical term applied to a disease that occurs over a large geographical area and that affects a high proportion of the population; a widespread epidemic.
Complete or partial loss of controlled movement caused by the inability to contract 1 or more muscles. Paralysis may be temporary or permanent. There may also be loss of feeling in affected areas.
A condition in which the central feature is the delusion that people or events are especially connected to oneself.
A neurological disorder that causes muscle tremor, stiffness, and weakness.
Involuntary inhalation of tobacco smoke by people who do not smoke.
The study of disease – its causes, mechanisms, and effects on the body.
The interpretation of a sensation.
The 2nd teeth, which usually start to replace the primary teeth at about the age of 6.
The sum of a person's traits, habits, and experiences.
The production and excretion of sweat from the sweat glands. Perspiration is another name for sweat.
Poisonous chemicals used to eradicate pests.

The abbreviation for positron emission tomography, a diagnostic technique based on the detection of positrons (a type of subatomic particle) that are emitted by radioactively labelled substances introduced into the body.
A measure of the acidity or alkalinity of a solution.
The branch of science that is concerned with the discovery and development of drugs; their chemical composition; their actions; their uses; and their side effects and toxicity.
The practice of preparing drugs, and making up and dispensing prescriptions. Also, a place where these activities are carried out.
The passage that connects the back of the mouth and nose to the oesophagus.
A persistent, irrational fear of, and desire to avoid, a particular object or situation.
An essential mineral, present in many foods, including cereals, dairy products, and meat. In the body, phosphorus is combined with calcium to form the bones and teeth.
Abnormal reaction to sunlight.
The study of body functions, including physical and chemical processes of cells, tissues, organs, and systems, and their various interactions.
Treatment with physical methods or agents.
A chemically inert substance given instead of a drug. Benefit may be gained from a placebo because the person taking it believes it will have a positive effect.
A serious infectious disease that is caused by the bacterium.
Any operation carried out to repair or reconstruct skin and tissue that has been damaged or lost, is malformed, or has changed with aging.
Inflammation of the lungs due to infection.
A growth that projects, usually on a stalk, from the lining of the nose, the cervix, the intestine, the larynx, or any other mucous membrane.
a compound consisting of many peptides
a tiny opening, usually in the skin
relating to the back of the body, or referring to the rear part.
care of the mother after childbirth until about 6 weeks later
A form of anxiety that develops after a stressful or frightening event.
vocabulary.cn: 创伤后精神紧张性障碍

vocabulary.definition: The relative position of different parts of the body at rest or during movement.
vocabulary.cn: 姿势, 仪态, 体位

vocabulary.definition: A mineral needed to help maintain normal heart rhythm, regulate the body's water balance, conduct nerve impulses, and contract muscles.
A term applied to any condition in which there is a tendency for cancer to develop.
Factors that lead to increased susceptibility to a disease.
The period from conception to birth.
happening or existing before birth
The progressive loss of the power of adjusting the eye for near vision.
An instruction written by a doctor that directs a pharmacist to dispense a particular drug in a specific dose.
Drugs and medicines that are not available over the counter and can only be obtained by prescription.
A substance that inhibits growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds and so protects foods from putrefying and fermenting.
The total number of cases of a disease at any one time in a defined population.
An aspect of dentistry concerned with the prevention of tooth decay and gum disease.

The branch of medicine that deals with the prevention of disease by public health measures, such as the provision of pure water supplies; by health education; by specific preventive measures, such as immunization against infectious diseases; and by screening programs to detect diseases before they cause symptoms.
A term applied to a disease that has originated within the organ or tissue affected, and is not derived from any other cause or source.
Health care provided by a general practitioner or other healthcare professional who is the 1st contact for a patient seeking medical treatment.
A tiny, protein-based infectious particle.
Species of microorganisms that inhabit the digestive tract, guarding it against harmful bacteria, yeasts, and viruses.
Inflammation of the rectum, causing soreness and bleeding, sometimes with a mucus and pus discharge.
An assessment of the probable course and outcome of a disease.
A term used to describe a condition that becomes more severe and/or extensive over time.
vocabulary.cn: （疾病等）进行性的, 愈来愈严重的

vocabulary.definition: A type of motor neuron disease in which the muscles of the hands, arms, and legs become weak and wasted and twitch involuntarily.
A solid, chestnut-shaped organ that surrounds the 1st part of the male urethra, just below the bladder.
Inflammation of the prostate gland, usually affecting men aged 30–50.
An artificial replacement for a missing or diseased part of the body.
vocabulary.cn: 假体 (如假肢、假眼或假牙)

vocabulary.definition: Large molecules consisting of hundreds or thousands of amino acids linked into long chains.
The formation of protein molecules through the joining of amino acids.
The simplest, most primitive type of animal, consisting of a single cell.
Prefixes meaning false.
A prefix meaning mental processes or activities, as in psychology.
The branch of medicine concerned with the study, prevention, and treatment of mental illness and emotional and behavioural problems.
A treatment based on psychoanalytic theory that can help people who have neuroses and personality disorders.
An aid to psychotherapy in which the patient acts out certain roles or incidents.
A term for a symptom or disorder that is caused by psychological or emotional problems.
The scientific study of mental processes.
Any operation on the brain that is carried out as a treatment for serious mental illness.
Treatment of mental and emotional problems by psychological methods.
Drugs that have an effect on the mind, including hallucinogenic drugs, sedative drugs, sleeping drugs, tranquillizer drugs, and anti-psychotic drugs.
The period when secondary sexual characteristics develop and the sexual organs mature.
Relating to the lungs.
Accumulation of fluid in the lungs, usually due to left-sided heart failure.
Scarring and thickening of lung tissue, usually as a result of previous lung inflammation.
A group of procedures used to evaluate lung function, to confirm the presence of some lung disorders, and to ensure that planned surgery on the lungs will not disable the patient.
The rhythmic expansion and contraction of an artery as blood is forced through it, pumped by the heart.
The circular opening in the center of the iris. In bright conditions, the pupil constricts; in dim light, it dilates.

A quality adjusted life year. QALY is used by health economists to compare costs and outcomes of treatment for various diseases. Each year of life saved or prolonged is adjusted by a factor, Q, which takes account of how close to normal is the individual's lifestyle before and after treatment.
An infectious fever caused by rickettsiae and transmitted to humans from cattle, sheep, and goats by unpasteurized milk.
vocabulary.cn: Q 热, 寇热, 昆士兰热

vocabulary.definition: A muscle with 4 distinct parts that is located at the front of the thigh and straightens the knee.
The isolation of a person or animal recently exposed to a serious infectious disease.
An acute viral infection of the nervous system, once known as hydrophobia, that primarily affects dogs but can be transmitted to humans by a bite or a lick over broken skin.
The emission of energy (as electromagnetic waves) or matter (as particles) from unstable atoms, which turns them into a more stable form.
Emitting or relating to the emission of ionizing radiation or particles.
vocabulary.cn: 放射性的; 放射性引起的

vocabulary.definition: The emission of alpha particles, beta particles, and/or gamma radiation that occurs when the nuclei of unstable atoms spontaneously disintegrate.
Hazards from radiation may arise from external sources of radiation or from radioactive materials taken into the body.
Extensive surgery that is aimed at eliminating a major disease, usually cancer, by removing affected tissue and surrounding tissue that might be diseased.
A term for anything that is almost transparent to radiation, especially to X-rays and gamma radiation.
A diagnostic technique based on detection of radiation emitted by radioactive substances introduced into the body.
Treatment of cancer and, occasionally, some noncancerous tumors, by X-rays or other radiation.
vocabulary.cn: 放射疗法, 放射治疗

vocabulary.definition: A form of controlled trial that evaluates the effectiveness of a drug, or other treatment, in which subjects are randomly allocated to one of the study groups.
A group of spots or an area of red, inflamed skin.
A term for any chemical substance that takes part in a chemical reaction.
Withdrawal of the gums from around the teeth, exposing part of the roots.
A general term for any sensory nerve cell (one that converts stimuli into nerve impulses).
Relating to or denoting heritable characteristics controlled by genes which are expressed in offspring only when inherited from both parents.
An action that occurs automatically and predictably in response to a particular stimulus, independent of the will of the individual.
An abnormal backflow of fluid in a body passage due to failure of the passage's exit to close fully.
A backflow of fluid. In medicine, the term is used to describe the return of swallowed food or drink from the stomach into the esophagus and mouth.

Treatment aimed at enabling a person to live an independent life following injury, illness, alcohol dependence, or drug dependence. Treatment may include physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and psychotherapy.
An immune response aimed at destroying organisms or substances that the body's immune system recognizes as foreign.
The recurrence of a disease after an apparent recovery, or the return of symptoms after a remission.
A temporary disappearance or reduction in the severity of the symptoms of a disease, or the period during which this occurs.
vocabulary.cn: （病情或疼痛的）缓解, 减退; 暂时康复

vocabulary.definition: Related to the kidney.
An overuse injury that affects keyboard workers and musicians, causing weakness and pain in the wrists and fingers.
A term for the processes by which oxygen reaches body cells and is utilized by them, and by which carbon dioxide is eliminated.
Sudden cessation of breathing, resulting from any process that severely depresses the function of the respiratory center in the brain.
The organs responsible for carrying oxygen from the air to the blood and expelling carbon dioxide.
Infection of the breathing passages, which extend from the nose to the alveoli.
The light-sensitive membrane that lines the back inner surface of the eye, and on which images are cast by the cornea and lens.
Separation of the retina from the outer layers at the back of the eye.
A popular term for any disorder that causes pain and stiffness in muscles and joints.
A type of arthritis in which the joints in the fingers, wrists, toes, or elsewhere in the body become painful, swollen, stiff, and, in severe cases, deformed.
Inflammation of the mucous membrane lining the nose, which may cause stuffiness, nasal discharge, and sneezing.
