Any of the flat, curved bones that form a framework for the chest and a protective cage around the heart, lungs, and other underlying organs.
A disease caused by nutritional deficiency that causes bone deformities in childhood.
Increased tone in one or more muscles, causing them to feel tight; the affected part of the body becomes stiff and inflexible.
The abbreviation for ribonucleic acid.
A dental procedure performed to save a tooth in which the pulp has died or become untreatably diseased, usually as the result of extensive dental caries.
A bag-like organ or body structure.
A solution of salt (sodium chloride).
The slightly alkaline fluid that is secreted into the mouth by the salivary glands and the mucous membranes lining the mouth.
Three pairs of glands that secrete saliva, via ducts, into the mouth.
A healing, soothing ointment.
A major vein that runs the length of the leg just under the skin.
A cancer of connective tissue. Types are osteosarcoma, Kaposi's sarcoma, chondrosarcoma, and fibrosarcoma.
A crust that forms on the skin or on a mucous membrane at the site of a healing wound or infected area.
A burn due to hot liquid or steam.
A surgical knife for cutting tissue.

Methods of producing images of organ structure (or sometimes function) using sound waves, radio waves, X-rays, or other forms of radiation. Techniques include ultrasound, CT, radionuclide, MRI, and PET scanning.
One of a pair of wing-shaped bones, commonly called the shoulder-blades, which are situated over the upper ribs at the back.
A general term for a group of psychotic illnesses that are characterized by disturbances in thinking, emotional reaction, and behavior.
Pain that radiates along the sciatic nerve.
The main nerve in each leg and the largest nerve in the body.
A medical term for hardening of a body tissue, usually used to refer to hardening of blood vessels (as in arteriosclerosis) or of nerve tissue (as occurs in the later stages of multiple sclerosis).
The testing of apparently healthy people with the aim of detecting disease at an early, treatable stage.

A form of depression in which mood changes occur with the seasons. Sufferers tend to become depressed in winter and feel better in spring. Exposure to bright light for 2–4 hours each morning seems to prevent occurrence in some people.

A term applied to a disease or disorder that results from or follows another disease (the primary disease). It also refers to a malignant tumor that has spread from a primary cancer elsewhere in the body.
The manufacture and release by a cell, gland, or organ of substances, (such as enzymes) needed for metabolic processes elsewhere in the body.
The use of a drug to calm a person.

A group of drugs used to produce sedation. Sedative drugs include sleeping drugs, antianxiety drugs, antipsychotic drugs, and some anti-depressant drugs. A sedative drug is often included in a premedication.
A hormone produced by the duodenum when acidic food enters it from the stomach.
A sudden episode of abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Recurrent seizures occur in epilepsy.
A term meaning old age or, more commonly, the decline in mental ability that may occur in old age.
The initial exposure of a person to an allergen or other substance recognized as foreign by the immune system, which leads to an immune response.
A region of the outer cerebrum of the brain in which sensory information comes to consciousness.
vocabulary.cn: 感觉皮质；感觉区域的大脑皮质

vocabulary.definition: The removal of normal external stimuli, such as sight and sound, from a person's environment.
Chiefly of a wound or a part of the body infected with bacteria.
Infection of a wound or body tissues with bacteria that leads to the formation of pus or to the multiplication of the bacteria in the blood.
A thin dividing wall within or between parts of the body.
A portion of diseased or dead tissue separated from, or joined abnormally to, surrounding healthy tissue.
A substance found in many tissues, particularly blood platelets, the digestive tract lining, and the brain.
The clear fluid that separates from blood when it clots. It contains salts, glucose, and proteins, including antibodies.
A reaction or consequence of medication or therapy that is additional to the desired effect.
A cavity in a bone, in particular one of the air-filled spaces in the bones surrounding the nose.
Inflammation of the membrane lining the facial sinuses caused by infection, usually spread from the nose.
A fast, but regular, heart-rate (more than 100 beats per minute) caused by increased electrical activity in the sinoatrial node.
The framework of bones that gives the body shape and provides attachment points for the muscles and underlying soft tissues of the body.
Irritation of the skin following contact with a specific substance that provokes an inappropriate or exaggerated reaction from the immune system.
Removal of a portion of skin for laboratory analysis in order to diagnose a skin disorder.
A technique used to repair areas of lost or damaged skin that are too large to heal naturally, that are slow to heal, or that would leave tethering or unsightly scars.
The bony skeleton of the head, which rests on the 1st cervical vertebra.
Insufficient sleep.
Walking while asleep.
A triangular bandage used to immobilize, support, or elevate an arm.
Dead tissue that has been shed from its original site; for example, loss of dead skin cells from the skin's surface.
A specimen for microscopic examination prepared by spreading a thin film of cells on to a glass slide.
A method of measuring visual acuity used during vision tests.
A mineral that helps to regulate the body's water balance and maintain normal heart rhythm and is involved in conduction of nerve impulses and contraction of muscles.
A chemical that is used to dissolve another substance.
A term meaning related to the body (soma), as opposed to the mind (psyche), or related to body cells, as opposed to germ cells (eggs and sperm).
A biosynthetic growth hormone given to children to treat short stature due to growth hormone deficiency.
A term used nominally to describe any disrupted area of the skin or mucous membranes, or adjectivally to describe an area that is tender or painful.
A rough or raw feeling in the back of the throat that causes discomfort, especially when swallowing.
An involuntary contraction of a muscle.
Increased rigidity in a group of muscles, causing stiffness and restriction of movement.
A sample of tissue, body fluids, waste products, or an infective organism taken for analysis, identification, and/or diagnosis.
A ring of muscle around a natural opening or passage that acts as a valve, regulating inflow or outflow.
An instrument used for measuring blood pressure.
A cylinder of nerve tissue that runs from the brain, down the central canal in the spine to the 1st lumbar vertebra.
A pulmonary function test used to diagnose or assess a lung disorder or to monitor treatment.

An organ that removes worn-out and defective red blood cells from the circulation and helps to fight infection by producing some of the antibodies, lymphocytes, and phagocytes that destroy invading microorganisms.
Tearing or stretching of the ligaments that hold together the bone ends in a joint, caused by a sudden pull.
The process of dyeing specimens of cells, tissues, or microorganisms in order for them to be clearly visible or easily identifiable under a microscope.
Slowing down or cessation of flow.
An instrument that is used for listening to sounds in the body, particularly those made by the heart or lungs.
A basic cell in the body from which more specialized cells are formed.
Tearing or stretching of muscle fibers as a result of suddenly pulling them too far.
Damage to part of the brain caused by an interruption to its blood supply.
The tissue that forms an organ's framework, as distinct from the functional tissue (the parenchyma) and the fibrous outer layer that holds the organ together.
A term describing mental events (such as thoughts) of which one is temporarily unaware but which can be recalled under the right circumstances.
The removal of unwanted fluid or semi-fluid material from the body with a syringe and hollow needle or with an intestinal tube and a mechanical pump.
A condition in which there is a lack of oxygen due to obstruction to the passage of air into the lungs.
Situated near the surface.
A second infection that occurs during the course of an existing infection.

The treatment of disease, injury, or other disorders by direct physical intervention, or those aspects of medicine that deal with the study, diagnosis, and management of disorders treated in this way.
A total or partial vulnerability to an infection or disorder.

A type of joint, found only between the bones of the skull, in which the adjacent bones are mobile during birth but then become so closely and firmly joined by a layer of connective tissue that movement between them is impossible.

A common symptom with various possible causes, including a foreign object in the throat; insufficient production of saliva; a disorder of the esophagus such as esophageal stricture; pressure on the esophagus, for example from a goiter; a nervous system disorder such as myasthenia gravis or stroke; or a psychological problem such as globus hystericus.
Structures deep within the skin that produce sweat, which is mainly water with some dissolved substances, including salt.
An indication of a disease or disorder that is noticed by the sufferer. By contrast, the indications that a doctor notes are called signs.

A condition in which stimulation of one of the senses (by a sound, for example) produces an additional response, such as the appearance of a color in addition to the normal perception associated with that stimulus.
A junction between 2 neurons across which a signal can pass. At a synapse, the 2 neurons do not come directly into contact but are separated by a gap called the synaptic cleft.
A group of symptoms and/or signs that, occurring together, constitutes a particular disorder.
A Chinese exercise system based on a series of over 100 postures between which slow, continuous movements are made. The aim is to exercise the muscles and integrate mind and body.
One of 10,000 specialized structures located mainly on the tongue, with some at the back of the throat and on the palate.
A class of lymphocyte.
vocabulary.cn: T 细胞；T 淋巴细胞

vocabulary.definition: The classification of certain characteristics of the tissues of prospective organ donors and recipients.
Inflammation of a tendon, usually caused by injury or overuse. Symptoms of tendinitis include pain, tenderness, and restricted movement.
A fibrous cord that joins muscle to bone or muscle to muscle. Tendons are strong and flexible, but inelastic.
Pain and tenderness on the outside of the elbow and in the back of the forearm.
Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, a technique intended to provide pain relief by applying electrodes to the skin to block impulses in underlying nerves.
vocabulary.cn: （用于减轻痛楚的）经皮神经电刺激

vocabulary.definition: The main androgen hormone.
One of 2 structures within the brain consisting of a walnut-sized mass of nerve tissue.
A term meaning related to treatment.
The treatment of any disease or abnormal physical or mental condition.
A technique by which temperature patterns on the surface of the skin are recorded in the form of an image.
An instrument used to measure temperature.
A blood clot that has formed inside an intact blood vessel.
One of the main endocrine glands, which helps to regulate the rate of all the body's internal processes.
The most important thyroid hormone.
A repeated, uncontrolled, purposeless contraction of a muscle or group of muscles, most commonly in the face, shoulders, or arms.
vocabulary.cn: (尤指面部或头部肌肉的) 抽搐

vocabulary.definition: A flat wooden or metal instrument used to hold the tongue on the floor of the mouth to allow examination of the throat.
One of a diverse group of remedies intended to relieve symptoms such as malaise, lethargy, and loss of appetite.
One of a pair of oval tissue masses at the back of the throat on either side.
Surgical removal of the tonsils, which is now performed only if a child suffers frequent, recurrent attacks of severe tonsillitis.
Inflammation of the tonsils as a result of infection.
A term describing a drug that is applied to the surface of the body, not swallowed or injected.
The property of being toxic (poisonous). Toxicity also refers to the severity of adverse effects or illness produced by a toxin, a poison, or a drug overdose.
A poisonous protein produced by pathogenic (disease-causing) bacteria, various animals, or some plants.
Minerals necessary in minute amounts in the diet to maintain health. Examples are chromium, copper, zinc, and selenium.
A radioactive substance that is introduced into the body so that its distribution, processing, and elimination from the body can be monitored.
A procedure in which part of the body is placed under tension to correct the alignment of 2 adjoining structures or to hold them in position.
Any characteristic or condition that is inherited (determined by 1 or more genes).
A sleeplike state in which consciousness is reduced, voluntary actions lessened or absent, and body functions diminished.
Drugs that have a sedative effect.
The unconscious displacement of emotions from people who were important during one's childhood, such as parents, to other people during adulthood.
A rearrangement of the chromosomes inside a person's cells.
A term meaning capable of being passed from one person, or one organism, to another.
Replacement of a diseased organ or tissue with a healthy, living substitute.
A physical injury or severe emotional shock.
Emergency treatment of patients suffering from acute trauma (such as severe and/or multiple injuries).
An involuntary, rhythmic, oscillating movement in the muscles of part of the body, most commonly those of the hands, feet, jaw, tongue, or head.
The muscle at the back of the upper arm.
The central part of the body, comprising the chest and abdomen.
An infectious disease, commonly called TB, caused in humans by the bacterium.
A term that describes any swelling but which is generally used to refer to an abnormal mass of tissue that forms when cells in a specific area reproduce at an increased rate.
An infectious disease contracted by eating food or drinking water contaminated with the bacterium.
An open sore appearing on the skin or on a mucous membrane that results from the destruction of surface tissue.
Sound with a frequency that is greater than the human ear's upper limit of perception: that is, higher than 20,000 hertz (cycles per second).
Invisible light from the part of the electromagnetic spectrum immediately beyond the violet end of the visible light spectrum.
A specific part of the mind in which ideas, memories, perceptions, or feelings that a person is not currently aware of are stored and processed.
Fats from plants rather than animals, which are better for one's health.
A waste product of the breakdown of proteins by the liver that is transported to the kidneys and eliminated in the urine.
Inflammation of the urethra, usually due to an infection but sometimes having other causes.
Of, relating to, or denoting the system of organs, structures, and ducts by which urine is produced and discharged, in mammals comprising the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra.
The hollow, muscular organ of the female reproductive system in which the fertilized ovum (egg) normally becomes embedded and in which the embryo and fetus develop.
The small, fleshy protuberance that hangs from the middle of the lower edge of the soft palate.
A form of immunization in which killed or weakened microorganisms, or inactivated bacterial toxins, are introduced into the body, usually by injection, to sensitize the immune system.
A preparation given to induce immunity against an infectious disease. Most vaccines contain the organisms (or parts of the organisms) against which protection is sought.
A structure that allows fluid or semi-fluid material to flow in 1 direction through a tube or passageway but closes to prevent reflux in the opposite direction.
Relating to the blood vessels.
Narrowing of blood vessels, causing reduced blood flow to a part of the body.
Widening of blood vessels, causing increased blood flow to a part of the body.
An animal that transmits a particular infectious disease.
Eating a diet that excludes meat and fish, and sometimes all other animal products.
A term that is sometimes used to describe a type of indefinite deep coma.
A vessel that returns blood towards the heart from the various organs and tissues of the body.
A common procedure in which a vein, usually in the forearm, is pierced with a needle to inject fluid or withdraw blood.
A diagnostic procedure that enables veins to be seen on an X-ray film after they have been injected with a substance opaque to X-rays.
The use of a machine called a ventilator to take over or assist breathing.
A device used for the artificial ventilation of a person who is unable to breathe naturally.
A cavity or chamber.
Any of the 33 approximately cylindrical bones that form the spine.
An illusion that one or one's surroundings are spinning.
A minute finger-like projection from a membranous surface.
An indication that a person is still alive.
Any of a group of complex organic substances that are essential in small amounts for the normal functioning of the body.
A common disorder of skin pigmentation in which patches of skin, most commonly on the face, hands, armpits, and groin, lose their color.
The performance of a surgical operation on a live animal, particularly for research purposes.
Two fibrous sheets of tissue in the larynx that are responsible for voice production.
Eject matter from the stomach through the mouth.
A common, contagious, harmless growth that occurs on the skin or mucous membranes.
vocabulary.cn: （病毒引起的皮肤上的）疣, 肉赘

vocabulary.definition: A disease caused by infective or parasitic organisms transmitted via water. Infections can be contracted if infected water is drunk, if it contaminates food, or if individuals swim or wade in it.
vocabulary.cn: 水媒传染病；介水传染病

vocabulary.definition: A condition that is caused by excessive water retention in the brain.
A raised bump on the skin that is paler than the adjacent tissue and which may be surrounded by an area of red inflammation.
Tissue in the nervous system composed of nerve fibers.
One of the 4 rearmost teeth, also known as 3rd molars.

Any type of wound is susceptible to the entry of bacteria; the resultant infection can delay healing, result in disability, and may even cause death. Infection of a wound is indicated by redness, swelling, warmth, pain, and sometimes by the presence of pus or the formation of an abscess.
A furrow in the skin. Wrinkling is a natural feature of aging and is caused by a loss of skin elasticity.
The joint between the hand and the arm that allows the hand to be bent forward and backward relative to the arm and also to be moved side to side.
Abnormal dryness of the mouth, which can cause bad breath and may predispose the sufferer towards tooth decay.

A form of electromagnetic radiation of short wavelength and high energy. X-rays are widely used in medicine for diagnosis and treatment because they can be used to image bones, organs, and internal tissues.
A naturally occurring carbohydrate that is only partially absorbed by the body and is sometimes used as a sweetener by people with diabetes.
An involuntary act, or reflex action, usually associated with drowsiness or boredom.
Types of fungi in which the body of the fungus comprises individual cells that occur either singly, in pairs, or in longer chains.
An infectious disease of short duration and variable severity that is caused by a virus transmitted by mosquitoes.
The 2 opposing and interdependent principles that are fundamental to traditional Chinese medicine and philosophy.
vocabulary.cn: 〔中国古代哲学概念中的〕阴阳

vocabulary.definition: A system of Hindu philosophy and physical discipline that is becoming increasingly popular throughout the world.

A trace element that is essential for normal growth, the development of the reproductive organs, normal functioning of the prostate gland, healing of wounds, and the manufacture of proteins and nucleic acids in the body.
Any infectious or parasitic disease of animals that can be transmitted to humans.
A drug used in the short-term treatment of insomnia.

A technique that is used in plastic surgery to change the direction of a pre-existing scar so that it can be hidden in natural skin creases, or to relieve skin tension caused by the contracture of a scar.
The cell that is produced when a sperm fertilizes an ovum.
